steps.title: Identify the currency problem
steps.content: The passage says confidence likely fell because Sidonian coins had dropped below the silver content widely considered suitable for trade. The coin from 367 BCE contained only 74.2 percent silver, and its yellowish appearance would have made the change visible to traders. The solution must therefore make the coins seem more trade-suitable while Sidon still has heavy financial obligations.

steps.title: Avoid adding more silver
steps.content: Because the city remains under financial pressure, the most logical fix is not to spend substantially more silver on each coin. If the amount of silver stays the same but the total coin weight decreases, the copper portion must shrink. That raises the percentage of silver in each coin even though the actual amount of silver is unchanged.

steps.title: Test the proposed actions
steps.content: Choice A would raise the required silver threshold, making the confidence problem harder to solve. Choice C keeps a similar low proportion of silver to copper, so heavier coins would still look unsuitable for trade. Choice D adds access to copper, but copper is associated with the very problem traders noticed. The action that keeps the silver amount consistent while decreasing the coin weight explains how confidence could be relieved without extra silver. The answer is B.
